title: Learn by solving, not by reading notes
description: You removed most traditional explanatory content and replaced it with problems, prompts, and guided self-learning. Students learn by solving, asking AI, and iterating rather than consuming faculty-written notes.
nature: Curriculum design; shifts faculty effort from content delivery to problem framing.
why: It is a direct answer to the question, "What should we still teach when AI can already generate the content?"
pick_for: Pick this if the goal is to change what students learn, what is dropped, and what becomes more important.

public_links.label: Read on blog: tools in data science jan 2026
Read on blog: tds jan 2026 ga1 released

title: Every question includes an "Ask AI" button
description: Every question can be sent directly to an LLM. Students can choose their preferred AI, ask for help at the moment of need, and the course treats that as normal behavior rather than cheating.
why: This changes the unit of learning from recall to AI-assisted problem solving and makes AI use observable rather than hidden.
pick_for: Pick this if the council wants to redesign how understanding is tested, graded, or defended in an AI-heavy environment.

title: Students are allowed to copy, collaborate, and even hack
description: You explicitly allow students to copy from peers, use AI heavily, and even hack questions where appropriate. The emphasis is not purity of process but resourcefulness, verification, and getting the job done.
why: This is one of the clearest departures from conventional academic norms and goes straight to the heart of what the institute now chooses to reward.
pick_for: Pick this if the council wants to redesign how understanding is tested, graded, or defended in an AI-heavy environment.

title: Teach what AI still cannot do well
description: Instead of emphasizing syntax or routine analysis, the course increasingly targets integration, prompting, debugging, validation, judgment, orchestration, and tool composition.
why: This is exactly the strategic curriculum question academic councils now face: what do we teach less of and more of in the AI era?
pick_for: Pick this if the goal is to change what students learn, what is dropped, and what becomes more important.

title: Use hard, messy problems to teach real-world problem solving
description: Questions may be intentionally tricky, partly wrong, hidden in the UI, out of syllabus, or only solvable by unusually resourceful students. The aim is to build adaptability, risk-taking, and real-world resilience.
why: It is a strong bet that inspiration and real-world readiness sometimes come from unusually hard problems, not smooth progression alone.
pick_for: Pick this if the council wants to redesign how understanding is tested, graded, or defended in an AI-heavy environment.

title: Test first, then teach from the mistakes

why: This is a scalable way to align teaching effort with actual misconceptions rather than assumed ones.
pick_for: Pick this if the goal is to change what students learn, what is dropped, and what becomes more important.

title: Test cases act like tutors, not just graders
category: Learning analytics, research, and faculty meta-skills
description: In your IITM BS Python OPPE work, the key pedagogical move is to make test cases diagnose specific mental-model failures - e.g. solving the visible examples instead of the problem, parsing by shape instead of contract, relying on brittle heuristics, or returning the wrong structure despite correct logic. why: This is one of the strongest bridges between AI, scale, and better learning rather than just faster grading.

title: Broken code is analyzed before it even compiles
category: Learning analytics, research, and faculty meta-skills
description: You are exploring `tree-sitter` style parsing so that even syntactically broken student code can still be inspected for logic and misconception patterns, enabling feedback before a clean compile.
